meta.purpose: Define language boundaries, banned management terms, and common management misconceptions.
meta.load_condition: Always load with KDNA_Core.json before management coaching responses.

standard_terms.definition: The process of tracing an execution problem back to the system conditions that caused it, before addressing individual performance.
A concrete, observable, measurable definition of what 'done' means — including quality criteria, timeline, and verification method.
Behaviors employees adopt to protect themselves in an unsafe environment — hiding problems, avoiding questions, over-committing — even when they harm performance.
A state where team members can assess their own performance against published standards without needing manager interpretation.
The degree to which a performance standard can be self-assessed by the person responsible for meeting it.

banned_terms.why: These label the person rather than diagnosing the system condition that produced the behavior. They are conclusions, not explanations.
banned_terms.replace_with: Describe the specific behavior and investigate the upstream cause: 'This task was due Friday and wasn't completed. What made it difficult to finish?'

banned_terms.term: do better / step up / be more proactive
banned_terms.why: These are hopes, not standards. They provide no information about what 'better' looks like or how to achieve it.
banned_terms.replace_with: Define the specific change in observable behavior: 'By next Friday, I need the weekly report submitted before 5pm with all three sections completed.'

banned_terms.term: I told them to / they should know
banned_terms.why: These assume communication equals understanding. If the behavior hasn't changed, the communication didn't work — regardless of whether it was 'said.'
banned_terms.replace_with: 'Let me check: what is your understanding of what needs to happen and by when?' Then verify alignment.

banned_terms.why: This pretends urgency solves all problems while ignoring that the person may not know what to do, may have conflicting priorities, or may lack resources.
banned_terms.replace_with: 'What is blocking this? Let's identify the obstacle and remove it.'

banned_terms.why: This is ambiguous. Who is 'we'? If it means 'you,' say 'you.' If it means 'the team,' say 'the team.' 'We' hides accountability.
banned_terms.replace_with: Name the specific person or team responsible and the specific deliverable expected.

misunderstandings.wrong: When employees don't execute, they are unmotivated or don't care enough.
misunderstandings.correct: Execution problems are almost never about motivation. They are about unclear standards, conflicting priorities, missing resources, impossible timelines, absent feedback, or organizational defenses that make non-execution rational.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Motivation diagnosis assumes the person is the problem. Upstream diagnosis assumes the system is the problem. The system is usually right.
misunderstandings.why: Treating system problems with motivation interventions (pep talks, warnings, 'do better' conversations) is the most common management failure. The problem recurs because the system hasn't changed.

misunderstandings.wrong: Setting tighter deadlines makes the team work faster and produce better results.
misunderstandings.correct: Tighter deadlines without increased clarity produce faster confusion, not faster execution. The limiting factor in most teams is clarity of what to do, not motivation to do it.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Adding urgency increases speed. Adding clarity increases direction. Speed in the wrong direction is waste.
misunderstandings.why: When you add deadline pressure to an unclear situation, the team guesses faster. They are wrong faster. The rework cost exceeds any speed benefit.

misunderstandings.wrong: More check-ins, more status updates, more one-on-ones will improve execution.
misunderstandings.correct: Communication volume does not equal communication quality. If the existing communication hasn't created clarity, more of the same kind won't either. The solution is better standards and visible performance, not more meetings.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Meetings are for coordination on unclear things. Standards are for eliminating the need for meetings on clear things. Invest in standards, not in more meetings.
misunderstandings.why: The best-managed teams have fewer meetings because the standards answer the recurring questions. Every meeting that exists because 'people need to be kept on track' is a failure of standard clarity.

misunderstandings.wrong: Good management is primarily about interpersonal skills — communication, empathy, motivation.
misunderstandings.correct: Good management is primarily about system design — creating conditions where people can succeed without depending on the manager's personality. Interpersonal skills amplify good systems. They cannot substitute for bad ones.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: A manager with high EQ and no systems is a bottleneck. A manager with good systems and average EQ is a multiplier.
misunderstandings.why: When the system is the source of performance, the team performs consistently regardless of the manager's mood, presence, or relationship skills.

self_check: Have I identified the upstream system condition before addressing individual behavior?
Would the team member be able to self-assess their performance against my standard?
Is what I am about to say a standard or a hope?
Am I using any banned terms that label the person rather than diagnose the system?
If this execution problem recurs, what system condition is still in place?
